raw_case:
Hemorrhagic Bullous Lichen Sclerosus of the Breast: A Rare Complication of Radiotherapy
Cutaneous side effects from radiotherapy are commonly reported in breast cancer patients. Radiation-induced hemorrhagic bullous lichen sclerosus (RHBLS) of the breast is a rare, but important, complication of radiotherapy. RHBLS typically presents as painful, hemorrhagic bullae with surrounding sclerotic tissue. A 71-year-old female with a history of whole breast radiotherapy for invasive ductal carcinoma of the right breast and ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) of the left breast presented to the clinic with pruritic, firm, and erythematous plaques involving the inframammary folds that progressed into large, painful, hemorrhagic bullae surrounded by porcelain-white skin over several weeks. Biopsy was consistent with RHBLS. She was initially treated with systemic steroids and Clobetasol 0.05% ointment twice daily with partial improvement. To the best of our knowledge, only three definitive cases have been previously reported in the literature making this the fourth case.

Introduction

Lichen sclerosus (LS) is a chronic inflammatory skin disease characterized by atrophic, white-to-porcelain papules and plaques that can cause significant pruritus and discomfort. Although genetic and autoimmune components have been suspected in the pathophysiology of LS, trauma-induced LS has also been reported [1,2]. The first report of LS affecting the breasts following radiotherapy was published in 1985 [3]. The first case of radiation-induced hemorrhagic bullous LS (RHBLS), a rare subtype of radiation-induced LS characterized by flaccid bullae and purpuric hemorrhage, involving the breasts was reported in 1994 [4]. Herein and to the best of our knowledge, we report the fourth known case of RHBLS of the breasts in a patient with a history of radiotherapy-treated breast cancer and emphasize the importance of early recognition, evaluation for genital involvement, and treatment to avoid further complications. As only three other cases have been documented, only limited conclusions can be drawn, emphasizing the importance of continued investigation into RHBLS.This article was previously presented as a meeting abstract at the American Academy of Dermatology's 2022 Gross and Microscopic Symposium.

Case presentation

A 71-year-old female with a history of invasive ductal carcinoma of the right breast (2011) and ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) with microinvasive carcinoma of the left breast (2019), cholelithiasis, hyperlipidemia, and carpal tunnel syndrome presented in March 2021 with pruritic, firm, and erythematous plaques in both inframammary folds. Treatment for suspected intertrigo with a combination of topical steroids, nystatin, and zinc oxide was unsuccessful. Over the ensuing weeks, the breasts, as well as the inframammary folds, developed progressively large, painful, hemorrhagic bullae surrounded by porcelain-white skin as noted on physical exam (Figure 1). She was noted to have sclerotic changes in regions outside the field of radiation, including the axillae and inguinal folds. For the invasive ductal carcinoma of the right breast, the patient underwent a partial mastectomy, followed by whole breast radiotherapy (4,800 grays (Gy) with 1,200 centiGrays (cGy) boost) in 2011 (Figure 2) and was treated with anastrozole from 2011 to 2016. In 2019 after an abnormal mammogram, she was found to have DCIS with microinvasive carcinoma of the left breast that was treated with lumpectomy, followed by whole breast radiotherapy (4,526 Gy with 1,000 cGy boost) of the left breast, as well as some lower radiation to the right breast (Figure 3), and letrozole therapy. Given the history of radiotherapy and physical exam findings, biopsies were performed to evaluate for RHBLS, bullous morphea, angiosarcoma, and chronic radiation dermatitis. The biopsies of a hemorrhagic bulla and sclerotic skin from the left breast resulted in a pauci-immune subepidermal bulla and lichen sclerosus, respectively (Figure 4). Taken collectively with the clinical exam, the patient was diagnosed with RHBLS. She was initially treated with systemic steroids and clobetasol 0.05% ointment bis in die (BID) with partial improvement at follow-up two months later.

Discussion

Cutaneous side effects from radiotherapy have been reported in up to 90% of breast cancer patients [5]. RHBLS of the breast is a rare, yet significant, complication of radiotherapy. To our knowledge, only three definitive cases have been previously reported in the literature [2,4,5] (Table 1). Trattner et al. reported a case of RHBLS of the breast of a 57-year-old female diagnosed with infiltrating ductal carcinoma three years after radiotherapy at a site of previously diagnosed morphea [4]. Peterson et al. reported a case of a 57-year-old female with unilateral RHBLS that presented three months after radiotherapy for invasive ductal carcinoma and eventually progressed to involve both breasts and vulva despite no radiotherapy to the left breast or vulva [5]. Bonfill-Ortí et al. reported RHBLS involving the right breast of a 71-year-old female two years after radiotherapy and letrozole for infiltrating the lobular carcinoma of the right breast [2]. Walsh et al. reported a case of hemorrhagic bullous LS in a 65-year-old woman with a history of breast cancer; however, there was no mention of any history of radiotherapy [6]. As radiotherapy is commonly used as primary and adjuvant treatment in cancer, it is likely that their case also represents RHBLS.RHBLS typically presents as painful, hemorrhagic bullae with surrounding sclerotic tissue. Involvement of skin outside the field of radiation, as noted in our patient, can be a feature seen in both radiation-induced LS and morphea but is atypical for other radiation-induced dermatoses [5,6]. If found to have RHBLS, a full body skin exam would be recommended to evaluate for other sites to rule out genital involvement [5]. We favored radiation as the most likely etiology as the lesions started on the breast prior to expanding to areas outside the radiation field as reported in the literature [5,6]; however, friction from wearing a bra could also be a contributory factor [5] but would not explain the involvement outside the treatment area. Clinically, RHBLS can overlap with bullous morphea including hemorrhagic bullae, necessitating a biopsy; however, porcelain-white skin is characteristic of LS. Histologically, morphea is characterized by excessive collagen deposition, which was not seen in our patient [4]. On histology, RHBLS is characterized by epidermal atrophy with orthokeratosis, a band of edematous, homogenized collagen in the papillary dermis with a dense degree of lymphocytic infiltrates, and subepidermal bullae [1,2].Regarding pathophysiology, koebernization from trauma, including radiation, has been reported [1,3,5]. Only Bonfill-Ortí et al. and our case report any treatment for breast cancer other than surgical intervention and radiation, suggesting that hormonal therapy is less likely to be the underlying trigger for RHBLS [2]. Radiation is thought to induce a TH2 immune response leading to increased fibroblast activity and sclerosis by upregulating IL-4 and TGF-beta, which can cause LS [5]. It is hypothesized the bullae that develop are because of alteration of the lymphatic vessels secondary to radiation [1]. The radiation regimen, including dose, fractionation, and boost, does not appear to correlate with the risk of developing RHBLS [5]. Treatment for RHBLS includes oral or high-potency topical corticosteroids as initial therapy [1,5]. Other therapies include topical calcineurin inhibitors, intralesional steroid injections, methotrexate, and phototherapy [1,2]. Symptoms and bullae have shown improvement in as little as three weeks [1].Other diagnoses to consider include chronic radiation dermatitis and angiosarcoma. Chronic radiation dermatitis can also present with atrophic areas after radiation, but histopathology shows atypical fibroblasts scattered singly in a background of thickened collagen bundles with superficially located dilated vessels containing swollen endothelial cells [7-9]. Angiosarcomas can occur postradiation and may present as violaceous plaques or nodules, but histopathology highlights an underlying irregular vascular process [9,10].

Conclusions

To our knowledge, this is the fourth definitive case of RHBLS reported. This report aims to raise awareness about RHBLS, a rare but distinct cutaneous sequela of radiotherapy. Given that radiotherapy is widely used as a primary and adjuvant treatment for cancer, early consideration of RHBLS is advised in patients with a history of radiotherapy who present with sclerotic skin and hemorrhagic bullae, even years after radiotherapy treatment. This case emphasizes the need for increased clinical vigilance and early intervention to prevent further complications. Given the small sample size of only four, including this one, reported cases in the literature, and limited conclusions, it is difficult to generalize the findings, so caution is needed when interpreting results. Continued research and collection of additional cases of RHBLS are necessary to strengthen the evidence and allow the development of clearer guidelines for the diagnosis and management of this condition. Larger case studies or systematic reviews would also help to better understand this condition.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 71-year-old female
- **Chief Complaint:** Pruritic, firm, and erythematous plaques in the inframammary folds that progressed into painful, hemorrhagic bullae surrounded by porcelain-white skin.
- **History of Present Illness:** The plaques developed over several weeks following whole breast radiotherapy for invasive ductal carcinoma of the right breast (2011) and ductal carcinoma in situ with microinvasive carcinoma of the left breast (2019). Initial treatment for presumed intertrigo with topical steroids, nystatin, and zinc oxide yielded no improvement. 
- **Past Medical History:** Invasive ductal carcinoma of the right breast (2011), ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) of the left breast with microinvasive carcinoma (2019), cholelithiasis, hyperlipidemia, and carpal tunnel syndrome. Received anastrozole (2011–2016) and letrozole (after 2019). 
- **Personal History:** Not mentioned.
- **Family History:** Not mentioned.
- **Physical Examination:** Large, painful, hemorrhagic bullae surrounded by porcelain-white skin over the breasts and inframammary folds, extending to areas outside the radiation field, including axillae and inguinal folds.
- **Ancillary Tests:** Biopsies performed on a hemorrhagic bulla and sclerotic skin revealed pauci-immune subepidermal bulla and histological features of lichen sclerosus without excessive collagen deposition seen in morphea.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **RHBLS (Radiation-Induced Hemorrhagic Bullous Lichen Sclerosus):**
   - Included based on the development of lesions at radiation-treated sites, expansion to adjacent areas, and biopsy findings of subepidermal bullae and histological features consistent with lichen sclerosus.
   - Porcelain-white skin was a characteristic finding.

2. **Bullous Morphea:**
   - Considered due to similar histological features and sclerosis but excluded based on the absence of excessive collagen deposition characteristic of morphea.

3. **Chronic Radiation Dermatitis:**
   - Excluded as histopathology in such cases typically shows atypical fibroblasts and dilated vessels with swollen endothelial cells, which were not present.

4. **Angiosarcoma:**
   - Excluded given the absence of irregular vascular pathology on histology.

Other considerations included friction-related trauma from bra use and non-radiation-related lichen sclerosus; however, the timing and location of lesions supported radiotherapy as the primary trigger.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's try to work through this case. The patient is a 71-year-old woman with a history of breast cancer, treated with radiotherapy and hormonal therapy. She's presenting with pruritic, firm, erythematous plaques in the inframammary folds that turned into painful hemorrhagic bullae surrounded by porcelain-white skin. The lesions started after radiotherapy and spread to areas outside the radiation field, like axillae and inguinal folds. Topical treatments for intertrigo didn't help.

First, I need to consider the differential diagnosis for bullous lesions in a patient with a history of radiation. Radiation dermatitis comes to mind, but the progression to bullae and spread beyond the radiation field might argue against that. The porcelain-white skin suggests possible lichen sclerosus, which the biopsy did show. But there's also a subepidermal bulla with pauci-immune features. Lichen sclerosus can sometimes lead to blistering, but maybe there's an autoimmune component here.

Another thought: Bullous pemphigoid. It's an autoimmune blistering disorder that can present with tense bullae. However, the presence of lichen sclerosus-like changes complicates things. Also, the biopsy shows pauci-immune, which might not fit classic pemphigoid, which usually has linear IgG/C3 deposits. But there are variants or overlap syndromes.

Morphea (localized scleroderma) was considered, but the biopsy didn't show excessive collagen deposition. So that's out. The history of anastrozole and letrozole use: could there be a drug-induced skin reaction? Some reports link aromatase inhibitors with lichenoid reactions, but combining that with bullae is less common.

Then there's radiation recall dermatitis, which can occur with certain chemotherapies, but she's on hormonal therapy. Also, the spread beyond radiation fields suggests a systemic process rather than a local reaction to radiation.

The combination of lichen sclerosus and subepidermal bullae makes me think of lichen sclerosus et atrophicus with bullous formation. Alternatively, maybe a coexisting autoimmune blistering disease. The pauci-immune aspect suggests possibly epidermolysis bullosa acquisita (EBA), which can be associated with trauma and presents with subepidermal blisters. EBA can sometimes be associated with other autoimmune conditions. However, EBA typically has more mechanical blistering, but the location in folds could be from friction.

Another angle: paraneoplastic pemphigus, but that's usually more severe with mucosal involvement, which isn't mentioned here. The patient's history of breast cancer might be a red herring, but paraneoplastic cases are possible. However, the biopsy didn't show the typical acantholysis.

Wait, the biopsy showed pauci-immune subepidermal bulla. So immune-mediated blistering diseases with minimal immune deposits: linear IgA bullous dermatosis, dermatitis herpetiformis, or maybe bullous lupus. But dermatitis herpetiformis is usually very pruritic with IgA deposits. Linear IgA can present with tense blisters but usually has IgA linear deposition. Bullous lupus has IgG and complement.

Alternatively, maybe a combination of lichen sclerosus and another blistering disorder. Since the biopsy on the bulla showed pauci-immune, maybe it's a localized blistering in the context of lichen sclerosus. Lichen sclerosus can sometimes lead to blistering due to severe dermal inflammation and sclerosis causing separation.

But there's a term called "lichen sclerosus with bullous lesions" or bullous lichen sclerosus. However, the extension to other areas like axillae and groin makes me think it's more widespread, possibly an autoimmune condition triggered by radiation or hormonal therapy.

Wait, there's a condition called "Bullous morphea," but the biopsy ruled out morphea. Alternatively, localized scleroderma with bullae, but again, the histology didn't support morphea.

The key here is the combination of lichen sclerosus and subepidermal bullae with minimal immune deposits. Maybe an overlap between lichen sclerosus and bullous pemphigoid? But the immune deposits would be expected in pemphigoid. Since the biopsy is pauci-immune, maybe it's a non-immune bullous disorder secondary to the lichen sclerosus.

Another possibility: the patient has both lichen sclerosus and a separate bullous disease. However, considering the clinical presentation and the biopsy findings, perhaps a diagnosis of lichen sclerosus complicated by bullous changes. But the presence of subepidermal bullae in a pauci-immune context might suggest epidermolysis bullosa acquisita. EBA is an autoimmune disorder targeting type VII collagen, which anchors the epidermis to the dermis. It can present with trauma-induced blisters, and the biopsy might show subepidermal blisters with minimal inflammation. Direct immunofluorescence (DIF) is key here. Pauci-immune might mean negative DIF, which can occur in EBA if the sample isn't taken properly or if it's a non-classic presentation.

Alternatively, mucous membrane pemphigoid can present with scarring and blisters, but again, DIF would show linear deposits. Since the biopsy here is described as pauci-immune, maybe the DIF was negative. This could point towards EBA, but often EBA has positive DIF with IgG at the dermal-epidermal junction. Wait, but "pauci-immune" might refer to the inflammatory infiltrate, not necessarily the DIF. The question is whether the ancillary tests included DIF. The case mentions biopsies showed "pauci-immune subepidermal bulla" — maybe meaning minimal inflammatory cells, and the histology of the skin showed lichen sclerosus. If DIF wasn't done, that complicates things. But perhaps in this case, the combination of lichen sclerosus and bullous lesions in a patient with history of radiation and hormonal therapy leads to a diagnosis of radiation-induced lichen sclerosus with bullous formation. Alternatively, a radiation-induced bullous disorder.

Wait, another angle: Radiotherapy can induce chronic skin changes, including lichen sclerosus-like lesions. Radiation-induced lichen sclerosus has been reported. The bullae could be due to severe sclerosis leading to blistering. But the extension beyond the radiation field is puzzling. Radiation typically affects the area irradiated. The axillae and inguinal folds being involved, which are outside the radiation field, suggests a systemic process. So maybe the radiation triggered an autoimmune response leading to widespread lichen sclerosus and bullous lesions. Alternatively, the hormonal therapy (aromatase inhibitors) could contribute to the development of lichen sclerosus and blistering. Letrozole and anastrozole are known to have skin side effects, including lichenoid reactions.

Putting it all together: The patient has lichen sclerosus (confirmed by biopsy) and subepidermal bullae. The bullous lesions could be a complication of lichen sclerosus, but they could also represent a concurrent autoimmune blistering disorder. However, the pauci-immune nature of the bulla (if referring to minimal immune complex deposition) might suggest that the blistering is secondary to the lichen sclerosus rather than a primary autoimmune bullous disease. Alternatively, the term "pauci-immune" could be indicating that there's no significant immune cell infiltrate in the bulla, which might align with a mechanical blister due to skin fragility from lichen sclerosus. 

But the porcelain-white skin is classic for lichen sclerosus, and the progression to hemorrhagic bullae in those areas could be due to the skin's fragility. However, the presence of lesions outside the radiation field suggests a systemic condition. Lichen sclerosus can be generalized, but it's more common in genital areas. The axillae and inguinal folds are areas prone to friction and moisture, which might be sites of lichen sclerosus, though less common.

Another possibility is that the patient has a combination of lichen sclerosus and another condition like bullous pemphigoid. The biopsy of the bulla showing subepidermal separation with minimal inflammation could be a very early lesion of bullous pemphigoid, but usually, there's eosinophilic infiltrate. However, the presence of lichen sclerosus might modify the histologic appearance. Alternatively, the patient has a drug-induced lichen sclerosus and bullous eruption due to letrozole or anastrozole. 

But the final answer here is likely a specific diagnosis. The biopsy results mention lichen sclerosus without features of morphea and a pauci-immune subepidermal bulla. Considering all this, the most probable diagnosis is lichen sclerosus with bullous formation. However, the term "bullous lichen sclerosus" is used when lichen sclerosus lesions develop bullae, typically due to severe inflammation and skin fragility. Alternatively, a diagnosis of lichen sclerosus complicated by bullous pemphigoid. But given the biopsy findings, if there's no immune deposition, then bullous lichen sclerosus is more likely. 

Another angle: The biopsy of the bulla shows subepidermal split with minimal inflammation (pauci-immune), and the other biopsy shows lichen sclerosus. The combination of these two could be a single disease process. Lichen sclerosus can lead to blistering due to interface dermatitis and dermal edema causing separation. Alternatively, the bullae could be a separate entity. However, given the clinical context and biopsy results, the diagnosis is likely bullous lichen sclerosus. 

Wait, but "bullous lichen sclerosus" is a rare manifestation. Another possibility is that the patient has both lichen sclerosus and a coexisting autoimmune blistering disease. However, without direct immunofluorescence findings, it's hard to confirm. But given the information provided, the answer is likely bullous lichen sclerosus. Alternatively, a diagnosis combining both findings: lichen sclerosus and a pauci-immune bullous disorder. But since the biopsies are from different sites (bullous and sclerotic skin), it's possible that the patient has two separate conditions. But the question is what's the final diagnosis based on the given data. The porcelain-white skin is classic for lichen sclerosus. The bullae in those areas and extending to other intertriginous areas could be due to the lichen sclerosus. 

Alternatively, maybe the patient has a chronic radiation dermatitis with lichen sclerosus-like changes and bullae formation. But the extension beyond radiation fields makes that less likely. 

Given all that, the most accurate diagnosis here is likely bullous lichen sclerosus.
results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient’s presentation includes pruritic plaques progressing to hemorrhagic bullae with porcelain-white skin, extending beyond radiation fields. Lichen sclerosus (LS) is confirmed histologically, and the bulla biopsy shows pauci-immune subepidermal separation. Radiation-induced LS is plausible, but extra-radiation involvement suggests systemic factors.  
<step 2> Bullous pemphigoid (BP) is considered due to tense bullae, but pauci-immune findings and lack of typical eosinophil-rich infiltrate or immune deposits on biopsy make BP less likely. Drug-induced blistering (e.g., from letrozole/anastrozole) is possible but lacks direct evidence.  
<step 3> The porcelain-white sclerotic skin and histology confirm LS. Bullae in LS are rare but documented in severe cases due to dermal inflammation/fragility. The pauci-immune bulla supports secondary blistering from LS rather than a primary autoimmune blistering disorder.  
<step 4> Exclusion of morphea (no excessive collagen) and radiation dermatitis (extra-field lesions) narrows the diagnosis. Coexisting conditions are unlikely given biopsy concordance and clinical progression.  

### Answer:
Bullous lichen sclerosus

reasoning_eval.step: The patient’s presentation includes pruritic plaques progressing to hemorrhagic bullae with porcelain-white skin, extending beyond radiation fields. Lichen sclerosus (LS) is confirmed histologically, and the bulla biopsy shows pauci-immune subepidermal separation. Radiation-induced LS is plausible, but extra-radiation involvement suggests systemic factors.

Bullous pemphigoid (BP) is considered due to tense bullae, but pauci-immune findings and lack of typical eosinophil-rich infiltrate or immune deposits on biopsy make BP less likely. Drug-induced blistering (e.g., from letrozole/anastrozole) is possible but lacks direct evidence.

The porcelain-white sclerotic skin and histology confirm LS. Bullae in LS are rare but documented in severe cases due to dermal inflammation/fragility. The pauci-immune bulla supports secondary blistering from LS rather than a primary autoimmune blistering disorder.

Exclusion of morphea (no excessive collagen) and radiation dermatitis (extra-field lesions) narrows the diagnosis. Coexisting conditions are unlikely given biopsy concordance and clinical progression.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: Included RHBLS based on lesions developing at radiation-treated sites, expanding to adjacent areas, and biopsy findings showing subepidermal bullae with histological features consistent with lichen sclerosus. Characteristic porcelain-white skin was observed.
Step 2: Considered Bullous Morphea due to similar histological features and sclerosis but excluded it due to the absence of excessive collagen deposition characteristic of morphea.
Step 3: Excluded Chronic Radiation Dermatitis as histopathology typically shows atypical fibroblasts and dilated vessels with swollen endothelial cells, which were not present.
Step 4: Excluded Angiosarcoma given the absence of irregular vascular pathology on histology.

Step 5: Noted other considerations including friction-related trauma from bra use and non-radiation-related lichen sclerosus, but the timing and location of lesions supported radiotherapy as the primary trigger.
Step 6: Final diagnosis: Radiation-Induced Hemorrhagic Bullous Lichen Sclerosus (RHBLS).
